title: Adversarially Weighted, Regime-Specific OOF Stacking With Sigmoid Meta-Blending
summary: The train/test split likely contains temporal and regime shift across years, drivers, and new regulations.
rationale: The train/test split likely contains temporal and regime shift across years, drivers, and new regulations. Adversarial validation is a standard competition tactic for selecting validation rows closer to test, and stacking works best when base learners capture different biases; sigmoid calibration preserves ranking while isotonic can change AUC via ties.
implementation_hint: Train a train-vs-test classifier to score test-likeness, use those scores to pick validation folds or sample weights, then stack diverse OOF models: baseline binary GBDT, ordered CatBoost, and the ranking model, optionally split into dry vs wet experts. Use a logistic meta-learner or sigmoid-only calibration on OOF predictions; avoid isotonic unless it empirically helps AUC.
expected_effect: Can reduce leaderboard/CV mismatch and convert several small orthogonal gains into a more stable public score lift.
risk: High implementation complexity and strong overfitting risk if the stack is not strictly OOF and race-grouped; adversarial weights can hurt if the detected shift is spurious.
